skills.prompt: This topic introduces the concept of accumulation for AP Calculus AB (CED: CHA-4.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.1). Accumulation of change is the idea that the total change in a quantity can be found by summing up small changes. If f(t) represents a rate of change, then the accumulation (total change) over [a,b] is the integral of f from a to b. Key concepts: connecting the area under a rate function to total change; if v(t) is velocity, the integral of v from a to b gives displacement; if R(t) is a production rate, the integral gives total production. Students should understand accumulation intuitively before formal Riemann sums: area under a positive rate curve represents total accumulation, and the net area (above minus below x-axis) gives net change. This provides motivation for the definite integral as a limit of sums. AP exam note: interpreting integrals as accumulated change in context is essential for free-response questions. Students must explain what the integral represents with correct units. Vocabulary: accumulation, total change, rate of change, area under curve, net change, integral.

This topic covers Riemann sums for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-5.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.2). Riemann sums approximate the area under a curve by dividing [a,b] into n subintervals and summing the areas of rectangles. Types: Left Riemann Sum (use left endpoint of each subinterval for height), Right Riemann Sum (use right endpoint), Midpoint Riemann Sum (use midpoint), and Trapezoidal Sum (average of left and right endpoints, forming trapezoids). For equal subintervals of width Delta_x = (b-a)/n: Left sum = Delta_x * sum of f(x_i) for left endpoints. Key concepts: as n increases, the approximation generally improves; for an increasing function, left sums underestimate and right sums overestimate; for a decreasing function, the reverse. The trapezoidal sum averages left and right sums. Students should compute Riemann sums from functions, tables, and graphs. AP exam note: Riemann sum calculations from tables of values are very common on free-response questions, often with unequal subintervals. Vocabulary: Riemann sum, left sum, right sum, midpoint sum, trapezoidal sum, subinterval, partition, overestimate, underestimate.

skills.skill: 6.3 Riemann Sums, Summation Notation, and Definite Integral Notation
skills.prompt: This topic covers the connection between Riemann sums and the definite integral for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-5.B, LIM-5.C, Unit 6, Topic 6.3). The definite integral is defined as the limit of Riemann sums: integral from a to b of f(x)dx = lim[n->infinity] sum[i=1 to n] f(x_i*) * Delta_x, where the partition gets arbitrarily fine. Key concepts: summation notation (sigma notation) for expressing Riemann sums compactly; the definite integral exists (the limit converges) when f is continuous on [a,b]; the definite integral represents the net signed area between f and the x-axis (positive above, negative below). Students should understand the integral symbol, limits of integration (a = lower, b = upper), the integrand f(x), and the differential dx. The definite integral is a number, not a function. AP exam note: students should connect Riemann sums to definite integrals and recognize that the definite integral gives exact net area. Vocabulary: definite integral, limit of Riemann sums, summation notation, net signed area, integrand, limits of integration, differential.

skills.skill: 6.4 The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus and Accumulation Functions
skills.prompt: This topic covers the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Part 1 (FTC1) for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-5.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.4). FTC1 states: if f is continuous on [a,b], then g(x) = integral from a to x of f(t)dt is continuous on [a,b], differentiable on (a,b), and g'(x) = f(x). In other words, the derivative of an accumulation function is the integrand evaluated at the upper limit. With the Chain Rule: d/dx[integral from a to h(x) of f(t)dt] = f(h(x)) * h'(x). Key concepts: the accumulation function g(x) gives the net area under f from a to x; g is increasing where f is positive and decreasing where f is negative; g has extrema where f changes sign; g has inflection points where f has extrema. This theorem connects differentiation and integration as inverse processes. AP exam note: analyzing accumulation functions from graphs of f is a classic free-response question. Students must relate properties of g to the graph of f. Vocabulary: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, accumulation function, integrand, upper limit, Chain Rule extension.

skills.skill: 6.5 Interpreting the Behavior of Accumulation Functions Involving Area
skills.prompt: This topic covers interpreting accumulation functions for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-5.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.5). Given g(x) = integral from a to x of f(t)dt, students analyze g using the graph of f. Key relationships: g(a) = 0 (the integral from a to a is zero); g is increasing where f > 0 and decreasing where f < 0; g has a relative maximum where f changes from positive to negative and a relative minimum where f changes from negative to positive; g is concave up where f is increasing and concave down where f is decreasing; g has inflection points where f has relative extrema. To find g at specific points, students compute the integral as net area (using geometric shapes like triangles, rectangles, and semicircles from the graph of f). AP exam note: this is one of the most frequently tested topics on the AP exam. Students must clearly connect their reasoning about g to features of the graph of f. Vocabulary: accumulation function, net area, increasing, decreasing, concavity, relative extrema, inflection point, geometric area.

This topic covers properties of definite integrals for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-6.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.6). Key properties: (1) integral from a to a of f(x)dx = 0; (2) integral from a to b of f(x)dx = -integral from b to a of f(x)dx (reversing limits changes sign); (3) integral from a to b of [f(x)+g(x)]dx = integral from a to b of f(x)dx + integral from a to b of g(x)dx (linearity); (4) integral from a to b of c*f(x)dx = c * integral from a to b of f(x)dx; (5) integral from a to b of f(x)dx + integral from b to c of f(x)dx = integral from a to c of f(x)dx (additivity over intervals). Students should use these properties to evaluate integrals by breaking them apart or combining them, and to compute integrals from given information about related integrals. AP exam note: questions frequently provide values of several integrals and ask students to compute a related integral using properties. Vocabulary: properties of integrals, linearity, additivity, reversing limits, constant multiple, sum rule.

skills.skill: 6.7 The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus and Definite Integrals
skills.prompt: This topic covers the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Part 2 (FTC2) for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-6.B, Unit 6, Topic 6.7). FTC2 states: if f is continuous on [a,b] and F is any antiderivative of f (F' = f), then integral from a to b of f(x)dx = F(b) - F(a). This provides a method to evaluate definite integrals by finding antiderivatives. Key concepts: F(b) - F(a) is written as F(x) evaluated from a to b, using the notation [F(x)] from a to b; any antiderivative works (the constant of integration cancels); this theorem transforms the problem of computing areas into finding antiderivatives. Example: integral from 0 to pi of sin(x)dx = [-cos(x)] from 0 to pi = -cos(pi) - (-cos(0)) = 1 + 1 = 2. AP exam note: students must find correct antiderivatives and evaluate accurately at both bounds. The Net Change Theorem follows: integral from a to b of f'(x)dx = f(b) - f(a). Vocabulary: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Part 2, antiderivative, evaluation, Net Change Theorem, bounds.

skills.skill: 6.8 Finding Antiderivatives and Indefinite Integrals: Basic Rules and Notation
skills.prompt: This topic covers basic antidifferentiation rules for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-6.C, Unit 6, Topic 6.8). The indefinite integral (antiderivative) of f(x) is written as integral of f(x)dx = F(x) + C, where F'(x) = f(x) and C is the constant of integration. Key rules: integral of x^n dx = x^(n+1)/(n+1) + C (n != -1); integral of 1/x dx = ln|x| + C; integral of e^x dx = e^x + C; integral of sin(x)dx = -cos(x) + C; integral of cos(x)dx = sin(x) + C; integral of sec^2(x)dx = tan(x) + C; integral of csc^2(x)dx = -cot(x) + C; integral of sec(x)tan(x)dx = sec(x) + C; integral of csc(x)cot(x)dx = -csc(x) + C; integral of 1/(1+x^2)dx = arctan(x) + C; integral of 1/sqrt(1-x^2)dx = arcsin(x) + C. Students should recognize these as the reverse of derivative formulas and always include +C for indefinite integrals. AP exam note: forgetting +C is a common error. These basic antiderivatives are the foundation for all integration techniques. Vocabulary: antiderivative, indefinite integral, constant of integration, reverse differentiation, basic integration rules.

This topic covers u-substitution for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-6.D, Unit 6, Topic 6.9). U-substitution reverses the Chain Rule: integral of f(g(x))*g'(x)dx = integral of f(u)du where u = g(x). Key steps: (1) identify the inner function u = g(x); (2) compute du = g'(x)dx; (3) rewrite the integral entirely in terms of u; (4) integrate with respect to u; (5) substitute back x. For definite integrals, either change the limits to u-values or substitute back after integrating. Students should recognize when substitution is appropriate: look for a composite function where the derivative of the inner function appears as a factor. Examples: integral of 2x*cos(x^2)dx with u = x^2; integral of e^(3x)dx with u = 3x (multiply/divide by constants as needed). AP exam note: u-substitution is one of the most frequently tested integration techniques. Students should be comfortable adjusting for missing constant factors. Vocabulary: u-substitution, change of variable, inner function, differential, reverse Chain Rule, change limits.

skills.skill: 6.10 Integrating Functions Using Long Division and Completing the Square
skills.prompt: This topic covers integration techniques involving long division and completing the square for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-6.D, Unit 6, Topic 6.10). Long division: when the degree of the numerator is greater than or equal to the degree of the denominator in a rational function, perform polynomial long division to rewrite the integrand as a polynomial plus a proper fraction, then integrate term by term. Example: integral of (x^2+1)/(x-1)dx: divide to get x + 1 + 2/(x-1), then integrate as x^2/2 + x + 2ln|x-1| + C. Completing the square: rewrite quadratic expressions in the denominator to match arctan or arcsin forms. Example: integral of 1/(x^2+4x+8)dx = integral of 1/((x+2)^2+4)dx = (1/2)arctan((x+2)/2) + C. Students should recognize when each technique is needed and execute the algebra accurately. AP exam note: these algebraic manipulations appear in multiple-choice and make otherwise intractable integrals computable. Vocabulary: long division, polynomial division, completing the square, proper fraction, arctan form, arcsin form.

This topic covers integration by parts for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-6.E, Unit 6, Topic 6.11). Integration by parts reverses the Product Rule: integral of u dv = uv - integral of v du. The LIATE guideline helps choose u (Logarithmic, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trigonometric, Exponential -- choose u from earlier in this list). Key applications: integral of x*e^x dx (u=x, dv=e^x dx), integral of x*sin(x)dx (u=x, dv=sin(x)dx), integral of ln(x)dx (u=ln(x), dv=dx), integral of x^2*e^x dx (apply twice). Sometimes integration by parts must be applied repeatedly, or the original integral reappears and can be solved algebraically. For definite integrals, apply the boundary evaluation to the uv term and the remaining integral. AP exam note: integration by parts appears on both AB and BC exams. Students should practice choosing u and dv efficiently and handling repeated applications. Vocabulary: integration by parts, LIATE, Product Rule reversal, tabular method, repeated application.

This topic covers partial fraction decomposition for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-6.F, Unit 6, Topic 6.12). When integrating rational functions p(x)/q(x) where q(x) factors into distinct linear factors, decompose into partial fractions: A/(x-r1) + B/(x-r2) + ... Then each term integrates to a natural logarithm. Key steps: (1) factor the denominator completely; (2) write the decomposition with unknown constants; (3) solve for the constants using algebraic methods (multiplying both sides by the denominator and substituting convenient values of x or equating coefficients); (4) integrate each term separately. Example: integral of (3x+1)/((x-1)(x+2))dx decomposes as A/(x-1) + B/(x+2). This technique applies when the degree of the numerator is less than the degree of the denominator; otherwise, perform long division first. AP exam note: the AP exam limits partial fractions to distinct linear (non-repeated) factors. Students should practice the full process from factoring through integration. Vocabulary: partial fractions, decomposition, linear factors, coefficients, rational function, natural logarithm.

This topic covers improper integrals for AP Calculus AB (CED: LIM-6.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.13). An integral is improper if: (1) one or both limits of integration are infinite, or (2) the integrand has an infinite discontinuity within [a,b]. Evaluation method: replace the infinite bound with a variable and take the limit. For type 1: integral from a to infinity of f(x)dx = lim[t->infinity] integral from a to t of f(x)dx. For type 2: if f has a discontinuity at c in [a,b], split into two improper integrals and evaluate each with a limit. If the limit exists and is finite, the integral converges; otherwise, it diverges. Key examples: integral from 1 to infinity of 1/x^p dx converges for p > 1 and diverges for p <= 1. Students should set up the limit, evaluate the integral, then take the limit. AP exam note: improper integrals appear in both AB and BC contexts. Students must show the limit process explicitly to receive full credit. Vocabulary: improper integral, convergent, divergent, infinite limit, discontinuity, limit process, p-integral.

This topic covers selecting appropriate integration techniques for AP Calculus AB (CED: FUN-6.D, FUN-6.E, FUN-6.F, Unit 6, Topic 6.14). Students must choose from: basic antiderivative rules, u-substitution, integration by parts, partial fractions, long division, or completing the square. Decision strategy: (1) check if the integrand matches a basic rule directly; (2) look for a composition suggesting u-substitution (inner function whose derivative is present); (3) for products of different function types, consider integration by parts; (4) for rational functions, check if partial fractions or long division applies; (5) for quadratics in denominators, try completing the square. Students should recognize integrands that match arctan and arcsin forms. Sometimes algebraic simplification or trigonometric identities are needed before applying a technique. AP exam note: the ability to quickly identify the correct technique is essential for time management. Practice with mixed problem sets is crucial. Vocabulary: technique selection, u-substitution, integration by parts, partial fractions, long division, completing the square, recognition.
